Frames the project as building an autonomous 'Junior Equity Research Analyst in a Box' — not a chatbot or RAG pipeline.

args.CodeContent:
# Step 1 — Requirement Analysis: AI Investment Research Agent

---

## 1. What the Assignment Is Actually Asking

At its core, this assignment asks you to build a **production-grade AI-powered system** that:

- **Ingests** financial data from multiple sources (market data APIs, SEC filings, news, earnings transcripts, etc.)
- **Reasons** over that data using an AI agent (not just a single LLM call — a multi-step, tool-using agent with planning capabilities)
- **Produces** structured, actionable investment research reports (comparable to what a junior analyst at a buy-side fund would write)
- **Presents** the results through an interactive frontend where users can query, explore, and drill down

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The keyword is **Agent**, not "chatbot" and not "RAG pipeline." Recruiters are testing whether you understand the difference. An agent autonomously plans, selects tools, gathers data, reasons, and synthesizes — all with minimal human guidance. A chatbot just answers questions. A RAG pipeline just retrieves and summarizes.

### The Implicit Product Being Built

Think of it as: **"A Bloomberg Terminal copilot that a portfolio manager can ask: 'Should I invest in NVIDIA right now?' and get back a structured, multi-source research report with a thesis, risk assessment, and comparable analysis — all generated autonomously by an AI agent."**

---

## 2. Explicit Requirements

| # | Requirement | Category |
|---|------------|----------|
| E1 | Build an AI agent (not a chatbot, not a simple RAG) | Core |
| E2 | The agent must perform investment research | Domain |
| E3 | Must use LangGraph or equivalent agentic framework | Tech |
| E4 | Must integrate with real financial data sources | Data |
| E5 | Must produce structured research output (reports) | Output |
| E6 | Must have a frontend interface | UI |
| E7 | Must have a backend API layer | Backend |
| E8 | Must demonstrate AI/ML engineering competence | Skill |
| E9 | Code must be production-quality (not a notebook prototype) | Quality |

---

## 3. Hidden Requirements (What They Don't Say But Expect)

> [!WARNING]
> These are the requirements that separate top 10% submissions from the rest.

| # | Hidden Requirement | Why It Matters |
|---|-------------------|----------------|
| H1 | **Error handling & graceful degradation** — What happens when an API is down? When the LLM hallucinates? When the ticker is invalid? | Shows production mindset |
| H2 | **Observability** — Logging, tracing, token usage tracking across the agent's multi-step execution | Shows you can debug and operate AI systems |
| H3 | **Cost awareness** — Token budgets, caching strategies, knowing when NOT to call the LLM | Shows you understand real-world AI economics |
| H4 | **Determinism & reproducibility** — Can you re-run the same query and get structurally consistent results? | Shows scientific rigor |
| H5 | **Data freshness & staleness** — How do you handle stale market data? Cache invalidation? | Shows domain understanding |
| H6 | **Prompt versioning** — Prompts are code. They should be versioned, tested, and not hardcoded inline | Shows engineering maturity |
| H7 | **Agent guardrails** — What prevents the agent from going into infinite loops? What caps iteration count? | Shows safety awareness |
| H8 | **Streaming responses** — Long research takes time. Users need to see progress, not a loading spinner for 45 seconds | Shows UX empathy |
| H9 | **Citation & source attribution** — Every claim in the report should link back to a source | Shows trustworthiness design |
| H10 | **Modular tool design** — Tools should be independently testable, swappable, and documented | Shows software architecture skills |

---

## 4. What Recruiters Are Evaluating

### Technical Evaluation Matrix

| Dimension | Weight | What They Look For |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|
| **AI/Agent Architecture** | 25% | Proper use of LangGraph, state management, tool orchestration, multi-step reasoning |
| **Software Engineering** | 25% | Clean code, separation of concerns, error handling, typing, testing |
| **System Design** | 20% | API design, database schema, scalability considerations, caching |
| **Domain Understanding** | 15% | Do you actually understand what a research report needs? Do the financial metrics make sense? |
| **Product Thinking** | 10% | UX quality, edge case handling, user flow completeness |
| **Communication** | 5% | README quality, documentation, code comments, commit messages |

### The Real Question They're Answering

> *"If I put this person on my AI product team on Monday, can they ship production AI features independently?"*

---

## 5. What Would Impress Reviewers

### Tier 1 — Expected (Gets You Past Screening)
- Working end-to-end flow: user enters ticker → agent researches → report displayed
- Clean code with type hints and docstrings
- Basic error handling
- README with setup instructions

### Tier 2 — Strong (Gets You an Interview)
- Multi-source data aggregation (not just one API)
- Agent reasoning trace visible in UI (show the "thinking" process)
- Streaming responses with real-time progress
- Proper state management in LangGraph (not just a linear chain)
- Unit tests for tools and critical paths
- Docker deployment

### Tier 3 — Exceptional (Gets You an Offer)
- **Comparative analysis**: Agent can compare two stocks, not just analyze one
- **Confidence scoring**: Agent rates its own confidence and explains data gaps
- **Human-in-the-loop**: User can intervene mid-research to redirect the agent
- **Memory/context**: Agent remembers prior research sessions
- **Cost dashboard**: Show token usage, API call count, estimated cost per report
- **Backtesting hooks**: Can the agent's historical recommendations be validated?
- **PDF export** with professional formatting
- **Real-time data refresh**: WebSocket-based live price updates
- **Multi-model routing**: Use cheaper models for simple tasks, powerful models for synthesis

---

## 6. Common Mistakes Most Candidates Make

| # | Mistake | Why It's Fatal |
|---|---------|---------------|
| M1 | **Building a chatbot, not an agent** — Just wrapping an LLM with a system prompt and calling it an "agent" | Fundamentally misses the assignment |
| M2 | **Hardcoded mock data** — Using fake stock data instead of real APIs | Shows you can't integrate real systems |
| M3 | **Single-shot LLM call** — No multi-step reasoning, no tool use, no planning loop | Not agentic |
| M4 | **No error handling** — App crashes on invalid ticker or API timeout | Not production-grade |
| M5 | **Ignoring the "research" part** — Outputting a paragraph of text instead of a structured research report with sections, metrics, and sources | Misses domain requirements |
| M6 | **Monolithic code** — Everything in one file, no separation of concerns | Poor engineering |
| M7 | **No tests** — Zero test coverage, not even for critical business logic | Unacceptable for senior roles |
| M8 | **Over-engineering** — Building a Kubernetes-deployed microservices system for a demo project | Shows poor judgment on scope |
| M9 | **Ignoring latency** — Agent takes 2+ minutes with no streaming, progress, or feedback | Bad UX |
| M10 | **No prompt engineering rigor** — Vague prompts, no few-shot examples, no output schema enforcement | Shows shallow AI understanding |
| M11 | **Hallucination-prone outputs** — No grounding, no fact-checking, no source attribution | Dangerous for financial domain |
| M12 | **Copy-pasted boilerplate** — Clearly template code with no customization | Reviewers recognize this immediately |

---

## 7. Functional Requirements

### Core Agent Capabilities
- [ ] FR-01: Accept a stock ticker or company name as input
- [ ] FR-02: Accept a natural language research question (e.g., "Is AAPL undervalued?")
- [ ] FR-03: Autonomously plan a research workflow (what data to gather, what analysis to perform)
- [ ] FR-04: Execute multi-step research with tool calls (not a single LLM invocation)
- [ ] FR-05: Aggregate data from multiple financial sources
- [ ] FR-06: Produce a structured research report with defined sections

### Data Retrieval
- [ ] FR-07: Fetch real-time and historical stock price data
- [ ] FR-08: Retrieve company financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow)
- [ ] FR-09: Fetch key financial ratios and metrics (P/E, EPS, ROE, debt-to-equity, etc.)
- [ ] FR-10: Retrieve recent news articles about the company
- [ ] FR-11: Access SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q) or earnings call summaries
- [ ] FR-12: Fetch peer/competitor data for comparative analysis
- [ ] FR-13: Retrieve sector/industry benchmarks

### Analysis & Reasoning
- [ ] FR-14: Perform fundamental analysis (valuation, growth, profitability)
- [ ] FR-15: Perform sentiment analysis on news and filings
- [ ] FR-16: Identify key risks and red flags
- [ ] FR-17: Generate an investment thesis (bull/bear case)
- [ ] FR-18: Provide a recommendation with confidence level
- [ ] FR-19: Support comparative analysis (stock vs. stock or stock vs. sector)

### Report Output
- [ ] FR-20: Generate report with: Executive Summary, Company Overview, Financial Analysis, Risk Assessment, Competitive Landscape, Investment Thesis, Recommendation
- [ ] FR-21: Include data tables with real numbers
- [ ] FR-22: Include source citations for every major claim
- [ ] FR-23: Support PDF/Markdown export

### Frontend
- [ ] FR-24: Search/input interface for ticker or research query
- [ ] FR-25: Real-time progress/streaming display during agent execution
- [ ] FR-26: Rendered research report view
- [ ] FR-27: Research history / saved reports
- [ ] FR-28: Interactive drill-down (click a metric to see source data)

### Backend API
- [ ] FR-29: REST/GraphQL endpoint to initiate research
- [ ] FR-30: WebSocket or SSE endpoint for streaming agent progress
- [ ] FR-31: Endpoint to retrieve past reports
- [ ] FR-32: Endpoint for health check and system status

---

## 8. Non-Functional Requirements

| Category | Requirement | Target |
|----------|------------|--------|
| **Performance** | Report generation latency | < 60 seconds for a standard single-stock report |
| **Performance** | API response time (non-agent) | < 200ms for cached data |
| **Scalability** | Concurrent users | Support at least 5-10 concurrent research requests |
| **Reliability** | Uptime | Graceful degradation when external APIs fail |
| **Reliability** | Agent loop safety | Max iteration cap (e.g., 15 steps) to prevent runaway |
| **Security** | API key management | No hardcoded secrets; use env vars or vault |
| **Security** | Input sanitization | Prevent prompt injection attacks |
| **Security** | Rate limiting | Protect against abuse on public endpoints |
| **Observability** | Logging | Structured logging with correlation IDs per research session |
| **Observability** | Tracing | Full agent execution trace (tool calls, LLM calls, decisions) |
| **Observability** | Metrics | Token usage, cost per request, latency percentiles |
| **Maintainability** | Code quality | Type hints, docstrings, linting (Ruff/ESLint), formatting |
| **Maintainability** | Modularity | Tools, agents, API, and UI as independent modules |
| **Testability** | Coverage | Unit tests for tools, integration tests for agent flows |
| **Cost** | Token efficiency | Caching, prompt optimization, model routing |
| **Compliance** | Financial disclaimers | "Not financial advice" disclaimers on all outputs |
| **Accessibility** | UI | WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, responsive design |

---

## 9. Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Impact if Wrong |
|---|-----------|-----------------|
| A1 | Free-tier financial APIs (yfinance, Alpha Vantage, FMP) are sufficient for demo | May need paid APIs or mock fallbacks |
| A2 | OpenAI/Anthropic API keys will be available for LLM calls | Need fallback to local models or mock |
| A3 | Users will input valid US stock tickers primarily | Need ticker validation and global market support |
| A4 | English-only interface and reports | Internationalization adds significant scope |
| A5 | Single-user demo (no multi-tenancy needed) | Auth/isolation needed if multi-user |
| A6 | Reports are point-in-time snapshots, not live-updating dashboards | Different architecture if real-time required |
| A7 | The agent needs to handle ~100 most common US equities well | Long-tail/OTC/penny stocks may have sparse data |
| A8 | LangGraph is the preferred agentic framework | Could use CrewAI, AutoGen, or custom; decision needed |
| A9 | PostgreSQL or SQLite is acceptable for persistence | NoSQL/vector DB adds complexity |
| A10 | The reviewer will run the app locally via Docker | Must have dead-simple setup |

---

## 10. Risks

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| R1 | **LLM hallucination** — Agent states false financial data | High | Critical | Ground every claim in retrieved data; add verification step |
| R2 | **API rate limits** — Free-tier APIs throttle during demo | High | High | Implement caching, fallback sources, graceful degradation |
| R3 | **Agent infinite loop** — Planning loop never converges | Medium | High | Max iteration cap, timeout, circuit breaker |
| R4 | **Stale data** — Cached data is outdated, report is misleading | Medium | High | TTL-based cache, freshness indicators in reports |
| R5 | **Cost overrun** — Agent uses too many tokens per request | Medium | Medium | Token budgets, model routing, prompt optimization |
| R6 | **Prompt injection** — User input manipulates agent behavior | Medium | High | Input sanitization, system prompt hardening |
| R7 | **Data source unavailability** — yfinance/Alpha Vantage API goes down | Medium | High | Multiple data sources, graceful fallback messaging |
| R8 | **Scope creep** — Building too many features, never finishing | High | Critical | Prioritize MVP, then layer features |
| R9 | **Financial misinformation liability** — Agent gives "buy" recommendation | Low | Critical | Add disclaimers, frame as analysis not advice |
| R10 | **Poor UX during long operations** — 60s+ wait with no feedback | High | Medium | Streaming, progress indicators, step-by-step updates |

---

## 11. Edge Cases

### Input Edge Cases
| # | Edge Case | Expected Behavior |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| EC1 | Invalid ticker (e.g., "XYZZZZ") | Return clear error: "Ticker not found" |
| EC2 | Delisted stock (e.g., former ticker) | Handle gracefully, show historical data if available |
| EC3 | Non-US stock (e.g., "TCS.NS") | Either support or clearly state "US equities only" |
| EC4 | ETF or index (e.g., "SPY", "^GSPC") | Handle differently — no earnings, different metrics |
| EC5 | Crypto ticker (e.g., "BTC-USD") | Either support or reject with clear messaging |
| EC6 | Ambiguous input (e.g., "Apple" vs "AAPL") | Disambiguation: "Did you mean AAPL (Apple Inc.)?" |
| EC7 | Empty or whitespace-only input | Validation error with helpful message |
| EC8 | Very long or injection-like input | Sanitize, truncate, reject malicious patterns |
| EC9 | Recently IPO'd company (sparse data) | Handle gracefully, note data limitations in report |
| EC10 | Penny stock / micro-cap (unreliable data) | Warn about data reliability |

### Processing Edge Cases
| # | Edge Case | Expected Behavior |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| EC11 | LLM returns malformed JSON/output | Retry with rephrased prompt; fallback to raw text |
| EC12 | One data source returns data, another doesn't | Partial report with noted data gaps |
| EC13 | Agent exceeds max iterations | Graceful termination with partial results |
| EC14 | Concurrent requests for the same ticker | Cache/deduplicate, don't double-fetch |
| EC15 | Market is closed (weekend/holiday data) | Use last available data, note market status |
| EC16 | Financial data has nulls/NaN | Handle missing fields; don't crash on division by zero |
| EC17 | Company has unusual fiscal year | Correctly handle non-calendar fiscal years |
| EC18 | News returns no results for obscure stock | Note absence, don't hallucinate news |

### Output Edge Cases
| # | Edge Case | Expected Behavior |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| EC19 | Report is too long for frontend display | Paginate or collapse sections |
| EC20 | Report contains conflicting data (bull vs bear signals) | Present both sides; don't force a single narrative |
| EC21 | User requests a second report immediately | Queue or process concurrently; don't block |

---

## 12. User Flows

### Flow 1 — Standard Research Query (Happy Path)
```
User opens app
  → Enters "NVDA" in search bar
  → Clicks "Generate Research Report"
  → Sees streaming progress: "Fetching price data... Analyzing financials... Reading news..."
  → Agent completes in ~45 seconds
  → Full research report rendered with sections, charts, and sources
  → User reads report, clicks "Export PDF"
```

### Flow 2 — Natural Language Query
```
User opens app
  → Types "Is Tesla overvalued compared to other EV makers?"
  → Agent parses intent: comparative analysis, TSLA vs RIVN/LCID/NIO
  → Performs multi-stock research
  → Generates comparative report with peer analysis table
```

### Flow 3 — Invalid Input
```
User enters "XYZZZZ"
  → Backend validates ticker → not found
  → Returns friendly error: "We couldn't find a stock with ticker XYZZZZ. Did you mean XYZ (Exxon)?"
  → User corrects and resubmits
```

### Flow 4 — API Failure / Degraded Mode
```
User enters "AAPL"
  → Agent starts research
  → News API returns 503
  → Agent logs warning, skips news analysis
  → Report generated with note: "⚠️ News analysis unavailable — news data source temporarily unreachable"
  → User still gets a useful partial report
```

### Flow 5 — Research History
```
User opens app
  → Sees sidebar with past research: "NVDA (2h ago)", "AAPL (yesterday)"
  → Clicks on past report
  → Report loads from database (not re-generated)
  → User can click "Refresh" to regenerate with latest data
```

### Flow 6 — Agent Observation / Transparency
```
User enters "MSFT"
  → During generation, UI shows agent's reasoning trace:
    Step 1: "Planning research approach for Microsoft Corp..."
    Step 2: "Calling tool: get_stock_price(MSFT)"
    Step 3: "Calling tool: get_financial_statements(MSFT)"
    Step 4: "Analyzing: P/E ratio of 35.2 is above sector average..."
    Step 5: "Calling tool: get_news(MSFT, days=7)"
    Step 6: "Synthesizing final report..."
  → User can see exactly what the agent did and why
```

### Flow 7 — Concurrent / Queued Requests
```
User submits "GOOG" research
  → While processing, submits "AMZN" research
  → UI shows both in queue/progress
  → Reports delivered as each completes
```

---

## 13. Suggested Production-Grade Features

> [!TIP]
> These are features that go **beyond the assignment** but fit naturally within its scope and would demonstrate senior-level product thinking.

### High-Impact, Moderate Effort
| Feature | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Agent Reasoning Trace UI** | Shows the agent's "thought process" — massively differentiating |
| **Multi-source confidence scoring** | "Data Quality: High (3/4 sources returned data)" |
| **Watchlist / Portfolio mode** | Save tickers, get periodic re-analysis alerts |
| **Cost & token usage dashboard** | Shows per-request cost — demonstrates production awareness |
| **Prompt playground** | Let reviewers modify analysis prompts and see impact |

### Medium-Impact, Lower Effort
| Feature | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Dark mode** | Expected in 2026 for any modern UI |
| **Report comparison** | Side-by-side diff of two reports on the same stock over time |
| **Keyboard shortcuts** | Power-user UX |
| **Structured output schemas** (Pydantic) | Guarantee report structure; never get malformed output |
| **Rate limit dashboard** | Show remaining API quota |

### High-Impact, Higher Effort
| Feature | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Multi-model routing** | GPT-4o for synthesis, GPT-4o-mini for summarization, local model for classification |
| **Human-in-the-loop checkpoints** | "Agent wants to compare against 5 peers. Proceed?" |
| **Backtesting module** | "What would have happened if you followed the agent's advice 6 months ago?" |
| **Voice input** | "Hey Agent, analyze Nvidia for me" |
| **Scheduled reports** | "Send me an AAPL analysis every Monday at 9am" |

---

## Summary: The Mental Model

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    What They Want                        │
│                                                         │
│   Not a chatbot.  Not a RAG pipeline.  Not a dashboard. │
│                                                         │
│   An AUTONOMOUS AGENT that:                             │
│   1. Understands what research is needed                │
│   2. Plans how to get the data                          │
│   3. Executes a multi-step research workflow            │
│   4. Reasons over the data                              │
│   5. Produces a professional research report            │
│   6. Explains its reasoning                             │
│   7. Handles failures gracefully                        │
│   8. Is wrapped in production-grade infrastructure      │
│                                                         │
│   Think: "Junior Equity Research Analyst in a Box"      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next Step**: Proceed to **Step 2 — Product Planning** to define the MVP scope, feature prioritization (MoSCoW), user personas, and product roadmap before touching any technical decisions.

args.CodeContent:
# Step 2 — Product Planning: AI Investment Research Agent

---

## 1. Product Vision

### One-Liner
> **An AI-powered investment research agent that autonomously analyzes stocks by gathering real-time financial data, reasoning across multiple sources, and producing professional-grade research reports — delivered through an elegant, transparent interface.**

### Problem Statement
Investment research is time-consuming, data-intensive, and requires synthesizing information from dozens of sources. A junior analyst spends 4-8 hours producing a single equity research report. Retail investors don't have access to professional-grade research tools at all.

### Solution
An autonomous AI agent that compresses that 4-8 hour workflow into under 60 seconds — while maintaining transparency about its reasoning, sources, and confidence level.

### Product Principles
| Principle | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **Autonomy over assistance** | The agent drives the research; the user steers, not rows |
| **Transparency over magic** | Show the reasoning, not just the result |
| **Grounded over creative** | Every claim tied to data; zero tolerance for hallucination |
| **Graceful over fragile** | Degrade elegantly; never crash silently |
| **Professional over casual** | Output should look like it came from a research desk |

---

## 2. User Personas

### Persona 1: The Portfolio Manager (Primary Power User)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Name** | Sarah Chen |
| **Role** | Portfolio Manager at a mid-size asset management firm |
| **Goal** | Quickly screen and evaluate investment ideas before deep-diving |
| **Pain Point** | Spends too much time on initial screening; needs a "first pass" analyst |
| **Usage Pattern** | Queries 3-5 tickers per day, reads executive summary first, drills into financials |
| **What She Values** | Speed, accuracy, structured format, source attribution, comparative analysis |
| **Quote** | *"I don't need the agent to make the decision — I need it to give me the right data, structured the right way, so I can make the decision faster."* |

### Persona 2: The Retail Investor (Secondary User)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Name** | James Rodriguez |
| **Role** | Self-directed retail investor, software engineer by day |
| **Goal** | Make informed investment decisions without paying for Bloomberg |
| **Pain Point** | Information overload; doesn't know which metrics matter or how to interpret them |
| **Usage Pattern** | Queries 1-2 tickers per week, reads the full report, wants plain-English explanations |
| **What She Values** | Clarity, education, risk warnings, jargon-free insights |
| **Quote** | *"I want to understand WHY a stock might be good or bad, not just see a wall of numbers."* |

### Persona 3: The Engineering Reviewer (Meta User — The Recruiter)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Name** | Alex Kim |
| **Role** | Senior AI Engineer evaluating your submission |
| **Goal** | Assess whether you can build production AI systems |
| **Pain Point** | Reviews 50+ submissions; most are chatbot wrappers or broken demos |
| **Usage Pattern** | `docker compose up`, enters "AAPL", reads the report, inspects the code, checks tests |
| **What They Value** | Clean architecture, agent reasoning visibility, error handling, test coverage, documentation |
| **Quote** | *"Show me you understand the difference between a demo and a product."* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Persona 3 is your most important user.** Every product decision should be filtered through: *"Would this impress an engineering reviewer who has seen 50 mediocre submissions today?"*

---

## 3. Feature Prioritization (MoSCoW)

### Must Have (MVP — Non-Negotiable)

| # | Feature | Rationale |
|---|---------|-----------|
| M1 | **Ticker input & validation** | Core entry point; must handle invalid input gracefully |
| M2 | **Multi-step agent with LangGraph** | The literal assignment requirement |
| M3 | **Stock price data retrieval tool** | Foundation of any financial analysis |
| M4 | **Financial statements retrieval tool** | Income statement, balance sheet, cash flow |
| M5 | **Key metrics/ratios calculation tool** | P/E, EPS, ROE, margins, etc. |
| M6 | **News retrieval tool** | Sentiment and current events context |
| M7 | **Company profile/overview tool** | Sector, industry, description, market cap |
| M8 | **Structured research report generation** | Agent synthesizes all data into formatted report |
| M9 | **Report sections**: Executive Summary, Company Overview, Financial Analysis, Risk Assessment, Investment Thesis | Minimum viable report structure |
| M10 | **Source citations in report** | Every claim grounded; non-negotiable for finance |
| M11 | **REST API backend** (FastAPI) | Clean API layer between frontend and agent |
| M12 | **Streaming/progress updates** (SSE/WebSocket) | Users must see progress during 30-60s generation |
| M13 | **Frontend with search and report display** | Must be visually polished, not a raw HTML page |
| M14 | **Agent reasoning trace display** | Show what the agent is doing and why |
| M15 | **Error handling & graceful degradation** | Invalid ticker, API failures, LLM errors |

### Should Have (Enhances Quality Significantly)

| # | Feature | Rationale |
|---|---------|-----------|
| S1 | **Research history / saved reports** | Shows persistence layer; professional UX |
| S2 | **SEC filings / earnings data tool** | Deeper fundamental analysis |
| S3 | **Peer comparison data** | "How does AAPL compare to MSFT and GOOG?" |
| S4 | **Confidence scoring** | Agent rates its own analysis quality |
| S5 | **Financial disclaimers** | "Not financial advice" — shows domain awareness |
| S6 | **Token usage / cost tracking** | Demonstrates production cost awareness |
| S7 | **PDF export** | Professional deliverable format |
| S8 | **Dark mode** | Expected in modern UI; easy win |
| S9 | **Docker Compose deployment** | One-command setup for reviewers |
| S10 | **Unit & integration tests** | Credibility for production readiness |

### Could Have (Differentiators)

| # | Feature | Rationale |
|---|---------|-----------|
| C1 | **Natural language query** ("Is Tesla overvalued?") | Shows NLU capability beyond ticker lookup |
| C2 | **Comparative analysis mode** (Stock A vs Stock B) | Advanced agent capability |
| C3 | **Interactive charts** (price history, metrics visualization) | Visual richness |
| C4 | **Human-in-the-loop checkpoints** | Advanced agentic pattern |
| C5 | **Multi-model routing** (expensive model for synthesis, cheap for extraction) | Cost optimization sophistication |
| C6 | **Watchlist / portfolio tracking** | Product completeness |
| C7 | **Agent memory across sessions** | Advanced LangGraph feature |
| C8 | **Prompt playground / configurability** | Reviewer engagement feature |

### Won't Have (Out of Scope)

| # | Feature | Why Excluded |
|---|---------|-------------|
| W1 | **Real-time trading execution** | Compliance nightmare; out of scope |
| W2 | **Multi-language support** | Scope creep; English-only is fine |
| W3 | **Mobile app** | Responsive web is sufficient |
| W4 | **User authentication / multi-tenancy** | Unnecessary for a demo/assignment |
| W5 | **Backtesting with historical performance** | Impressive but too time-consuming for MVP |

---

## 4. MVP Definition — "Done" Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MVP is complete when a reviewer can do the following end-to-end flow without encountering any errors:

```
1. Clone repo
2. Run `docker compose up` (or `npm run dev` + `python main.py`)
3. Open browser to localhost:3000
4. Enter "NVDA" in the search bar
5. See streaming progress: "Fetching price data... Analyzing financials..."
6. See agent reasoning trace updating in real-time
7. Within 60 seconds, see a complete research report with:
   - Executive Summary
   - Company Overview  
   - Financial Analysis (with real numbers in tables)
   - Risk Assessment
   - Investment Thesis (bull & bear case)
   - Recommendation with confidence level
   - Source citations
8. Enter "XYZZZZ" — see a clear, friendly error
9. Check the README — clear setup instructions, architecture diagram, tech stack
10. Run `pytest` — tests pass
```

---

## 5. Delivery Roadmap

### Phase 1 — Foundation & MVP (Days 1-5)

```
Day 1: Project setup, folder structure, database schema, API skeleton
Day 2: Build all data retrieval tools (price, financials, news, profile)
Day 3: LangGraph agent — state machine, planning node, tool execution, synthesis
Day 4: Backend API (FastAPI) — endpoints, streaming, error handling
Day 5: Frontend MVP — search, streaming progress, report rendering
```

**Deliverable**: Working end-to-end flow (M1-M15)

### Phase 2 — Enhancement & Polish (Days 6-9)

```
Day 6: Research history, persistence layer, PDF export
Day 7: Peer comparison, confidence scoring, cost tracking
Day 8: UI polish — dark mode, animations, responsive design, charts
Day 9: Testing — unit tests, integration tests, edge case handling
```

**Deliverable**: Should-Have features (S1-S10)

### Phase 3 — Differentiation & Documentation (Days 10-12)

```
Day 10: Natural language queries, comparative analysis mode
Day 11: Security hardening, prompt injection protection, observability
Day 12: Documentation, README, architecture diagrams, deployment guide
```

**Deliverable**: Could-Have features (C1-C3) + production hardening

---

## 6. Success Metrics & KPIs

### Product Metrics (For the Demo)

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Time to first report** | < 60 seconds | Timer from submit to report render |
| **Report completeness** | 100% of sections populated | Automated check |
| **Data accuracy** | 0 hallucinated financial numbers | Manual spot-check against Yahoo Finance |
| **Error recovery rate** | 100% of API failures handled gracefully | Test with network throttling |
| **Setup time for reviewer** | < 3 minutes from clone to running app | Test on a fresh machine |

### Technical Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Test coverage** | > 70% on core tools and agent logic | `pytest --cov` |
| **Token usage per report** | < 15,000 tokens (input + output) | LangGraph callback tracking |
| **Estimated cost per report** | < $0.10 with GPT-4o-mini / < $0.50 with GPT-4o | Token tracking |
| **Agent step count** | 5-12 steps per report (not 1, not 50) | LangGraph trace |
| **API response time** (non-agent) | < 200ms | FastAPI middleware |

---

## 7. Competitive Positioning

> Where does this product sit relative to existing tools?

| Tool | What It Does | Our Differentiator |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Bloomberg Terminal** | Professional-grade data + analytics | We're free, AI-powered, and produce narrative reports — not just data screens |
| **Morningstar** | Research reports by human analysts | We generate reports in 60 seconds, not 2 weeks |
| **ChatGPT / Claude** | Can discuss stocks conversationally | We use **real-time data with tools**, not training data cutoffs. Structured output, not chat |
| **Finviz / Yahoo Finance** | Data dashboards and screeners | We provide **synthesis and narrative**, not just numbers on a screen |
| **Composer / Magnifi** | AI-powered investment platforms | We're transparent about reasoning; open architecture; not a black box |

### Our Unique Value Proposition
```
"The only AI investment research tool that shows you exactly how it thinks,
 what data it used, and how confident it is — in under 60 seconds."
```

---

## 8. Risk-Adjusted Scope Strategy

> [!CAUTION]
> The biggest risk is **scope creep**. The roadmap above is aggressive. Here's the fallback strategy:

### If Running Behind Schedule

| Priority | Cut This | Keep This |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| First cut | PDF export, charts, dark mode | Core agent, streaming, report |
| Second cut | Research history, peer comparison | Single-stock analysis, error handling |
| Never cut | Tests, README, Docker setup | These are what reviewers check first |

### The Iron Rule
> **A polished MVP with 15 features is infinitely better than a broken prototype with 30 features.**

---

## Summary: What We're Building

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                      │
│   Product: AI Investment Research Agent               │
│                                                      │
│   Input:  Stock ticker or research question          │
│   Output: Professional research report (60 seconds)  │
│                                                      │
│   Key Differentiators:                               │
│   ✦ Autonomous multi-step agent (not a chatbot)      │
│   ✦ Real-time data from multiple sources             │
│   ✦ Transparent reasoning trace                      │
│   ✦ Grounded analysis with source citations          │
│   ✦ Production-grade infrastructure                  │
│                                                      │
│   Target: Impress a senior AI engineer reviewer      │
│   in under 3 minutes of interaction                  │
│                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next Step**: Proceed to **Step 3 — Research Investment Domain** to build deep domain knowledge about equity research, financial metrics, report structures, and data source APIs before making any technical decisions.

args.CodeContent:
# Step 3 — Research Investment Domain

> **Goal**: Build the domain knowledge needed to make informed technical decisions. Every schema, prompt, API choice, and report template in later steps will be grounded in this research.

---

## 1. Anatomy of a Professional Equity Research Report

A professional sell-side or buy-side research report follows a predictable structure. Our agent must produce output that mirrors this format.

### Standard Report Sections

| # | Section | Purpose | Data Sources | Priority |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | **Executive Summary** | 3-5 sentence TL;DR with recommendation | Synthesized from all sections | Must |
| 2 | **Company Overview** | What the company does, sector, market cap, key stats | Company profile API | Must |
| 3 | **Investment Thesis** | Bull case + Bear case with supporting evidence | Synthesized by LLM from data | Must |
| 4 | **Financial Analysis** | Revenue, earnings, margins, growth trends | Financial statements API | Must |
| 5 | **Valuation Analysis** | Is the stock cheap or expensive? Relative & absolute valuation | Ratios + peer comparison | Must |
| 6 | **Risk Assessment** | What could go wrong? Macro, company-specific, sector risks | News + filings + LLM reasoning | Must |
| 7 | **Competitive Landscape** | Key competitors, market position, moat analysis | Peer data + LLM synthesis | Should |
| 8 | **Technical Indicators** | Price trends, 52-week range, moving averages, volume | Price data API | Should |
| 9 | **Recent News & Catalysts** | Material events, earnings surprises, product launches | News API | Must |
| 10 | **Recommendation** | Buy / Hold / Sell with confidence level and price target rationale | Synthesized | Must |

### Example Executive Summary (What "Good" Looks Like)

> **NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) — BUY | Confidence: High**
>
> NVIDIA continues to dominate the AI accelerator market with ~80% data center GPU market share. FY2025 revenue grew 122% YoY to $130.5B, driven by explosive demand for H100/H200 GPUs from hyperscalers and enterprises. At a forward P/E of 35.2x (vs. semiconductor sector average of 22.1x), the stock trades at a premium, but we believe this is justified by: (1) 40%+ projected revenue growth in FY2026, (2) expanding software margins via CUDA ecosystem lock-in, and (3) the $1T+ TAM of AI infrastructure. Key risks include export controls to China, AMD/custom silicon competition, and potential capex slowdown from hyperscalers. Our 12-month price target is $165, representing 18% upside from current levels.
>
> *Sources: NVIDIA 10-K (FY2025), Yahoo Finance, Reuters, Semiconductor Industry Association*

---

## 2. Key Financial Metrics — The Complete Reference

### Valuation Metrics

| Metric | Formula | What It Tells You | Good Range* | Red Flag |
|--------|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------|
| **P/E Ratio** (Price-to-Earnings) | Share Price / EPS | How much investors pay per $1 of earnings | 15-25x (varies by sector) | Negative (losses) or > 50x |
| **Forward P/E** | Share Price / Estimated Future EPS | Expected valuation based on projections | Lower than trailing P/E = growth expected | Much higher than trailing = growth slowing |
| **P/S Ratio** (Price-to-Sales) | Market Cap / Revenue | Useful for unprofitable companies | 1-5x | > 20x (unless hypergrowth) |
| **P/B Ratio** (Price-to-Book) | Market Cap / Book Value | Value vs. assets | 1-3x | < 1 (distressed) or > 10x |
| **PEG Ratio** | P/E / Earnings Growth Rate | P/E adjusted for growth | ~1.0 is "fair" | > 2.0 (overvalued for growth) |
| **EV/EBITDA** | Enterprise Value / EBITDA | Valuation independent of capital structure | 8-15x | > 25x |
| **EV/Revenue** | Enterprise Value / Revenue | Useful for high-growth, low-profit companies | 2-8x | > 20x |
| **Dividend Yield** | Annual Dividend / Share Price | Income return to shareholders | 1.5-4% | > 8% (sustainability risk) |

*Ranges are generalizations; always compare to sector peers.

### Profitability Metrics

| Metric | Formula | What It Tells You | Healthy Range |
|--------|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Gross Margin** | (Revenue - COGS) / Revenue | Pricing power and production efficiency | > 40% (tech), > 20% (retail) |
| **Operating Margin** | Operating Income / Revenue | Core business profitability | > 15% is strong |
| **Net Margin** | Net Income / Revenue | Bottom-line profitability after all costs | > 10% is strong |
| **EBITDA Margin** | EBITDA / Revenue | Cash-flow profitability (capital-structure neutral) | > 20% is strong |
| **ROE** (Return on Equity) | Net Income / Shareholder Equity | How well the company uses investor capital | > 15% is strong |
| **ROA** (Return on Assets) | Net Income / Total Assets | How efficiently assets generate profit | > 5% is decent |
| **ROIC** (Return on Invested Capital) | NOPAT / Invested Capital | True return on all capital deployed | > WACC = value creation |

### Growth Metrics

| Metric | Formula | What It Tells You |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **Revenue Growth (YoY)** | (Current Rev - Prior Rev) / Prior Rev | Top-line momentum |
| **Earnings Growth (YoY)** | (Current EPS - Prior EPS) / Prior EPS | Bottom-line momentum |
| **Free Cash Flow Growth** | Change in FCF YoY | Cash generation trend |
| **Quarterly Revenue Growth (QoQ)** | Sequential quarter comparison | Short-term momentum |
| **Revenue CAGR (3Y/5Y)** | Compound annual growth rate | Long-term growth trajectory |

### Liquidity & Solvency Metrics

| Metric | Formula | What It Tells You | Healthy Range |
|--------|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Current Ratio** | Current Assets / Current Liabilities | Can the company pay short-term obligations? | 1.5 - 3.0 |
| **Quick Ratio** | (Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities | Liquidity without selling inventory | > 1.0 |
| **Debt-to-Equity** | Total Debt / Shareholder Equity | Financial leverage | < 1.0 (conservative), < 2.0 (acceptable) |
| **Interest Coverage** | EBIT / Interest Expense | Can the company service its debt? | > 5x is comfortable |
| **Free Cash Flow** | Operating Cash Flow - CapEx | Actual cash available after operations | Positive and growing |
| **Cash per Share** | Cash & Equivalents / Shares Outstanding | Cash buffer per share | Context-dependent |

### Efficiency Metrics

| Metric | Formula | What It Tells You |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **Asset Turnover** | Revenue / Total Assets | How efficiently assets generate revenue |
| **Inventory Turnover** | COGS / Average Inventory | How fast inventory sells |
| **Receivables Turnover** | Revenue / Avg Accounts Receivable | How fast the company collects payment |

### Market/Trading Metrics

| Metric | What It Tells You |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Market Cap** | Total market value (price × shares outstanding) |
| **52-Week High/Low** | Trading range over the past year |
| **Beta** | Volatility relative to the market (1.0 = market average) |
| **Average Volume** | Liquidity / trading activity |
| **Short Interest** | Percentage of shares sold short (bearish sentiment) |
| **Institutional Ownership %** | "Smart money" conviction |

---

## 3. Valuation Methodologies

Our agent should understand (and reference) these approaches:

### 3.1 Relative Valuation (Peer Comparison)
```
"NVDA trades at 35x forward P/E vs. semiconductor peer median of 22x"
```
- Compare the stock's multiples to its industry peers
- Most common and practical for our agent
- **Our agent MUST support this** — it's the minimum viable valuation approach

### 3.2 DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) — Simplified
```
Intrinsic Value = Sum of (Future FCF / (1 + discount rate)^n) + Terminal Value
```
- The "textbook" approach; hard to do well with an LLM
- **Our agent should acknowledge it but not attempt a full DCF** — too many assumptions
- Can reference simplified versions: "At a 10% discount rate with 20% FCF growth, a rough DCF suggests fair value around $X"

### 3.3 Comparable Transactions
- What did acquirers pay for similar companies?
- Relevant for M&A targets
- **Nice to have**, not MVP

### 3.4 Dividend Discount Model (DDM)
- Only relevant for dividend-paying stocks
- **Skip for MVP** — too narrow

### 3.5 Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP)
- For conglomerates with diverse business segments
- **Skip for MVP** — too complex

> [!TIP]
> **For our agent, the primary valuation approach should be Relative Valuation (peer comparison).** It's data-driven, doesn't require speculative assumptions, and is the approach most commonly used by analysts for quick assessments.

---

## 4. Data Sources & APIs

### Free-Tier APIs (Primary — Use These)

| API | Data Provided | Rate Limit | Auth | Quality |
|-----|--------------|------------|------|---------|
| **yfinance** (Python library) | Price, financials, profile, holders, options | No official limit (scrapes Yahoo) | None | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good for most needs |
| **Alpha Vantage** | Price, fundamentals, forex, crypto, news/sentiment | 25 requests/day (free) | API key | ⭐⭐⭐ Solid but rate-limited |
| **Financial Modeling Prep (FMP)** | Financials, ratios, SEC filings, peers, earnings | 250 requests/day (free) | API key | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Best free fundamental data |
| **Finnhub** | Price, news, financials, SEC filings, ESG | 60 calls/min (free) | API key | ⭐⭐⭐ Good news coverage |
| **FRED (Federal Reserve)** | Macro data (GDP, CPI, interest rates, unemployment) | 120 requests/min | API key | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Gold standard for macro |
| **SEC EDGAR** | 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K filings (full text) | 10 requests/sec | User-Agent header | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Official source |
| **NewsAPI** | News articles from 80K+ sources | 100 requests/day (free) | API key | ⭐⭐⭐ Good for news retrieval |
| **Google News RSS** | News headlines | No official limit | None | ⭐⭐ Headlines only, no full text |

### Recommended Data Source Strategy

```
Primary:   yfinance        → Price data, financials, company profile, key stats
Secondary: FMP (free tier)  → Financial ratios, peer list, earnings calendar
Tertiary:  Finnhub/NewsAPI  → News and sentiment
Macro:     FRED             → Interest rates, inflation (for context)
Filings:   SEC EDGAR        → 10-K/10-Q text (if needed for deep analysis)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **yfinance is the backbone.** It provides 80% of the data we need with zero authentication and no rate limits. FMP fills in the gaps (ratios, peers). News comes from Finnhub or NewsAPI.

### Data Source Reliability Matrix

| Data Point | Primary Source | Fallback Source | Cache TTL |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Current price | yfinance | Alpha Vantage | 5 min |
| Historical prices | yfinance | Alpha Vantage | 24 hours |
| Income statement | yfinance | FMP | 24 hours |
| Balance sheet | yfinance | FMP | 24 hours |
| Cash flow statement | yfinance | FMP | 24 hours |
| Key ratios | Calculated from above | FMP | 24 hours |
| Company profile | yfinance | FMP | 7 days |
| News | Finnhub | NewsAPI | 1 hour |
| Peer/competitor list | FMP | Hardcoded top-5 by sector | 7 days |
| SEC filings | SEC EDGAR | FMP | 30 days |
| Macro data | FRED | Hardcoded recent values | 24 hours |

---

## 5. Financial Statement Relationships

Understanding how the three statements connect is crucial for prompt engineering.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  INCOME STATEMENT                       │
│  (Performance over a period)                            │
│                                                         │
│  Revenue                                                │
│  - Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)                           │
│  ─────────────────────────                              │
│  = Gross Profit           → Gross Margin                │
│  - Operating Expenses (R&D, SGA)                       │
│  ─────────────────────────                              │
│  = Operating Income (EBIT) → Operating Margin           │
│  - Interest Expense                                     │
│  - Taxes                                                │
│  ─────────────────────────                              │
│  = Net Income              → Net Margin, EPS            │
│         │                                               │
│         ▼                                               │
│  Flows into → Retained Earnings (Balance Sheet)         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   BALANCE SHEET                         │
│  (Snapshot at a point in time)                          │
│                                                         │
│  ASSETS = LIABILITIES + EQUITY                          │
│                                                         │
│  Current Assets          │ Current Liabilities          │
│  - Cash                  │ - Accounts Payable           │
│  - Receivables           │ - Short-term Debt            │
│  - Inventory             │                              │
│                          │ Long-term Liabilities        │
│  Non-Current Assets      │ - Long-term Debt             │
│  - Property/Equipment    │ - Bonds Payable              │
│  - Goodwill/Intangibles  │                              │
│                          │ Shareholder Equity           │
│                          │ - Common Stock               │
│                          │ - Retained Earnings ←────────│
│                          │                              │
│         │                                               │
│         ▼                                               │
│  Feeds into → Cash Flow Statement                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                CASH FLOW STATEMENT                      │
│  (Cash movement over a period)                          │
│                                                         │
│  Operating Cash Flow (OCF)                              │
│  = Net Income + Non-cash adjustments + Working capital  │
│                                                         │
│  Investing Cash Flow                                    │
│  = CapEx + Acquisitions + Asset sales                  │
│                                                         │
│  Financing Cash Flow                                    │
│  = Debt issuance/repayment + Dividends + Buybacks      │
│                                                         │
│  Free Cash Flow (FCF) = OCF - CapEx                    │
│  ──────────────────────────                             │
│  THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT NUMBER for many investors   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 6. How Professional Analysts Think — The Research Workflow

This mental model should inform our agent's planning logic.

### Step-by-Step Analyst Workflow

```
1. SCREEN
   "Is this stock worth researching?"
   → Quick check: market cap, sector, recent performance, any catalysts?

2. UNDERSTAND THE BUSINESS
   "What does this company actually do?"
   → Business model, revenue segments, competitive position, management quality

3. ANALYZE FINANCIALS
   "Is the company financially healthy and growing?"
   → 3-5 years of income statement, balance sheet, cash flow trends
   → Calculate margins, growth rates, return metrics

4. ASSESS VALUATION
   "Is the stock cheap or expensive?"
   → Compare multiples to peers and historical averages
   → Sanity-check with simplified DCF or growth-adjusted metrics

5. IDENTIFY RISKS
   "What could go wrong?"
   → Company-specific risks (key customer concentration, regulatory, etc.)
   → Industry risks (competition, disruption, cyclicality)
   → Macro risks (interest rates, recession, geopolitics)

6. REVIEW CATALYSTS
   "What could move the stock in the next 6-12 months?"
   → Earnings reports, product launches, M&A, regulatory decisions

7. FORM A THESIS
   "What's my view and why?"
   → Bull case (what goes right) + Bear case (what goes wrong)
   → Assign conviction level

8. WRITE THE REPORT
   "Communicate the thesis clearly with supporting evidence"
   → Structured format, data tables, source citations
```

> [!TIP]
> **Our LangGraph agent should roughly mirror this workflow.** Each step becomes a node or set of tool calls in the agent's state machine.

---

## 7. Sector-Specific Considerations

Different sectors have different key metrics. Our agent should be sector-aware.

| Sector | Key Metrics to Emphasize | Unique Considerations |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Technology** | Revenue growth, gross margin, R&D spend, rule of 40 | High P/E is normal; focus on growth |
| **Financials (Banks)** | NIM, loan loss provisions, CET1 ratio, ROE | Standard P/E less useful; use P/B, P/TBV |
| **Healthcare/Pharma** | Pipeline drugs, FDA approval status, patent cliffs | Revenue can be binary (drug approval) |
| **Energy** | Proved reserves, production growth, breakeven oil price | Highly cyclical; tied to commodity prices |
| **Consumer Staples** | Dividend yield, payout ratio, organic growth | Defensive; valued for stability |
| **Real Estate (REITs)** | FFO, AFFO, cap rate, occupancy rate, NAV | Use P/FFO not P/E; required 90% payout |
| **Industrials** | Backlog, book-to-bill ratio, capacity utilization | Cyclical; watch economic indicators |
| **Utilities** | Dividend yield, payout ratio, rate base growth | Regulated; predictable but slow growth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For MVP, we should detect the company's sector and adjust which metrics are highlighted.** A one-size-fits-all analysis will look amateur.

---

## 8. Regulatory & Compliance Considerations

| Requirement | Implementation |
|-------------|---------------|
| **"Not financial advice" disclaimer** | Every report must include: *"This report is generated by an AI agent for informational purposes only. It does not constitute financial, investment, or trading advice."* |
| **Data attribution** | All data must cite its source (Yahoo Finance, SEC, etc.) |
| **No forward-looking guarantees** | Language must use "may", "could", "potential" — never "will" or "guaranteed" |
| **Timeliness disclosure** | Report should state when data was last fetched |
| **AI-generated disclosure** | Must disclose that content is AI-generated |

---

## 9. Sentiment & Qualitative Signals

Beyond numbers, analysts assess qualitative factors. Our agent should incorporate:

| Signal | How to Detect | Data Source |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **News sentiment** | NLP sentiment analysis on recent headlines | News API + LLM |
| **Earnings surprise history** | Did the company beat/miss estimates? | FMP or yfinance |
| **Insider trading** | Are executives buying or selling? | Finnhub, SEC Form 4 |
| **Analyst consensus** | Average rating and price targets from analysts | yfinance |
| **Institutional ownership changes** | Are big funds increasing or decreasing positions? | yfinance |
| **Social sentiment** | Reddit/Twitter buzz (if accessible) | Nice to have |

---

## 10. Essential Financial Glossary

> Terms the agent must understand and use correctly in reports.

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **EPS** | Earnings Per Share — Net income divided by shares outstanding |
| **P/E Ratio** | Price-to-Earnings — How much investors pay for each dollar of earnings |
| **Market Cap** | Share price × total shares outstanding |
| **Enterprise Value (EV)** | Market cap + debt - cash — total company value |
| **EBITDA** | Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation & Amortization |
| **Free Cash Flow (FCF)** | Operating cash flow minus capital expenditures |
| **Gross Margin** | (Revenue - COGS) / Revenue — measures production efficiency |
| **Operating Margin** | Operating income / Revenue — core business profitability |
| **ROE** | Return on Equity — Net income / shareholder equity |
| **Beta** | Stock's volatility relative to the overall market |
| **Book Value** | Total assets minus total liabilities |
| **Dividend Yield** | Annual dividend / share price |
| **Payout Ratio** | Dividends / Net income — sustainability of dividend |
| **Debt-to-Equity** | Total debt / shareholder equity — leverage measure |
| **Current Ratio** | Current assets / current liabilities — short-term health |
| **Revenue Growth (YoY)** | Year-over-year revenue change percentage |
| **CAGR** | Compound Annual Growth Rate — smoothed annual growth |
| **MOAT** | Competitive advantage that protects against competitors |
| **TAM** | Total Addressable Market — maximum potential market size |
| **Capex** | Capital Expenditures — spending on long-term assets |
| **WACC** | Weighted Average Cost of Capital — discount rate for DCF |
| **Short Interest** | Shares sold short / total float — bearish sentiment indicator |
| **Float** | Shares available for public trading |
| **Dilution** | Increase in shares outstanding (stock options, secondaries) |
| **Buyback** | Company repurchasing its own shares — reduces share count |

---

## 11. Report Quality Checklist

> Use this to validate every report the agent produces.

- [ ] **Every number has a source** — No uncited financial figures
- [ ] **Numbers are recent** — Data timestamp visible; not stale
- [ ] **Metrics match the sector** — P/E for tech, P/FFO for REITs, etc.
- [ ] **Bull AND bear case present** — Not one-sided
- [ ] **Risks are specific** — Not generic "market risk" — actual company-specific risks
- [ ] **Recommendation has rationale** — Not just "Buy" but "Buy because X, Y, Z"
- [ ] **Confidence level justified** — High/Medium/Low with explanation of data gaps
- [ ] **Peer comparison included** — At least 2-3 peers with key metric comparison
- [ ] **Disclaimer present** — "Not financial advice" + AI disclosure
- [ ] **Language is professional** — No casual tone, no speculation presented as fact

---

## Summary: Domain Knowledge Map

```
                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │  INVESTMENT      │
                    │  RESEARCH AGENT  │
                    └────────┬─────────┘
                             │
          ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
          │                  │                  │
    ┌─────▼─────┐    ┌──────▼──────┐    ┌──────▼──────┐
    │  DATA      │    │  ANALYSIS   │    │  OUTPUT     │
    │  LAYER     │    │  LAYER      │    │  LAYER      │
    └─────┬─────┘    └──────┬──────┘    └──────┬──────┘
          │                  │                  │
    • yfinance         • Fundamental      • Report Sections
    • FMP              • Valuation        • Data Tables
    • Finnhub/News     • Sentiment        • Citations
    • SEC EDGAR        • Risk ID          • Disclaimers
    • FRED             • Peer Compare     • Visualizations
                       • Thesis Gen       • PDF Export
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next Step**: Proceed to **Step 4 — Tech Stack Planning** to select specific technologies for each layer: backend framework, frontend framework, database, LLM provider, agentic framework, and deployment tooling.

args.CodeContent:
# Step 4 — Tech Stack Planning

> **Goal**: Select and justify every technology choice. Each decision should be defensible in an interview: *"Why did you choose X over Y?"*

---

## 1. Technology Decision Framework

Every choice is evaluated on 5 criteria:

| Criteria | Weight | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|
| **Fit for purpose** | 30% | Does it solve the specific problem well? |
| **Industry adoption** | 20% | Is it widely used? Will reviewers recognize it? |
| **Developer experience** | 20% | Fast to build with? Good docs? |
| **Production readiness** | 15% | Stable, secure, performant? |
| **Interview signal** | 15% | Does it show you know modern best practices? |

---

## 2. Stack Overview

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      TECH STACK                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Frontend      │  Next.js 15 + React 19 + TypeScript        │
│  Styling       │  Tailwind CSS 4 + shadcn/ui                │
│  State         │  React Server Components + Zustand          │
│  Charts        │  Recharts                                   │
│                │                                             │
│  Backend       │  FastAPI (Python 3.12)                      │
│  Validation    │  Pydantic v2                                │
│  Streaming     │  Server-Sent Events (SSE)                   │
│                │                                             │
│  AI/Agent      │  LangGraph + LangChain                      │
│  LLM           │  OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (primary)               │
│                │  OpenAI GPT-4o (synthesis)                   │
│  Observability │  LangSmith                                  │
│                │                                             │
│  Data Sources  │  yfinance + FMP + Finnhub + NewsAPI         │
│  Database      │  PostgreSQL (prod) / SQLite (dev)           │
│  ORM           │  SQLAlchemy 2.0 + Alembic                   │
│  Cache         │  Redis (optional) / In-memory LRU           │
│                │                                             │
│  Testing       │  pytest + pytest-asyncio + Vitest           │
│  Linting       │  Ruff (Python) + ESLint (TS)                │
│  Formatting    │  Ruff (Python) + Prettier (TS)              │
│  Deployment    │  Docker Compose                             │
│  CI            │  GitHub Actions                             │
│                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 3. Detailed Technology Decisions

### 3.1 Backend Framework — FastAPI

| | FastAPI | Django | Flask | Express.js |
|---|---------|--------|-------|------------|
| **Async native** | ✅ Built-in | ⚠️ ASGI add-on | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Auto API docs** | ✅ Swagger/OpenAPI | ❌ Manual | ❌ Manual | ❌ Manual |
| **Pydantic integration** | ✅ First-class | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Streaming (SSE)** | ✅ StreamingResponse | ⚠️ Awkward | ⚠️ Awkward | ✅ |
| **Python ecosystem** | ✅ LangChain, yfinance | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ Node.js |
| **Performance** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Interview signal** | 🔥 Modern, impressive | 😐 Expected | 😐 Dated | 😐 Wrong language |

> **Decision: FastAPI** — Async-native, Pydantic-first, auto-generates API docs, and is the industry standard for Python AI backends. The entire LangChain/LangGraph ecosystem is Python, making this the natural choice.

### 3.2 Frontend Framework — Next.js 15

| | Next.js 15 | Vite + React | Svelte | Vue |
|---|-----------|-------------|--------|-----|
| **SSR/RSC** | ✅ React Server Components | ❌ SPA only | ✅ SvelteKit | ✅ Nuxt |
| **Ecosystem** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Largest | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Streaming support** | ✅ Native Suspense | ⚠️ Manual | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| **Routing** | ✅ App Router (file-based) | ❌ Manual | ✅ | ✅ |
| **TypeScript** | ✅ First-class | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Interview signal** | 🔥 Industry standard | 😐 Fine | 🤔 Niche | 😐 Fine |

> **Decision: Next.js 15** — App Router with React Server Components, built-in API routes as a BFF proxy, streaming-first architecture with Suspense, and the most recognizable React framework for reviewers.

> [!NOTE]
> We use Next.js purely as the frontend layer. The AI agent and business logic live entirely in the FastAPI backend. Next.js acts as the UI + BFF (Backend for Frontend).

### 3.3 Styling — Tailwind CSS 4 + shadcn/ui

| | Tailwind + shadcn/ui | Vanilla CSS | MUI | Chakra UI |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-----|-----------|
| **Speed of development** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Design quality** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Premium feel | ⭐⭐⭐ Manual effort | ⭐⭐⭐ Material look | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Customizability** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Full control | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Bundle size** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Tiny | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ Heavy | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Interview signal** | 🔥 Modern standard | 😐 | 😐 Enterprise | 😐 |

> **Decision: Tailwind CSS 4 + shadcn/ui** — shadcn/ui provides beautifully designed, accessible, copy-paste React components built on Radix UI primitives. Combined with Tailwind, we get a premium dark-mode UI with minimal effort. Components are owned (not imported from node_modules), making them fully customizable.

### 3.4 AI Agent Framework — LangGraph

| | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen | Custom |
|---|----------|--------|---------|--------|
| **State machine** | ✅ First-class graphs | ❌ Role-based | ❌ Chat-based | 🔨 Build from scratch |
| **Tool orchestration** | ✅ Native | ✅ | ✅ | 🔨 Manual |
| **Streaming** | ✅ astream_events | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | 🔨 Manual |
| **Human-in-the-loop** | ✅ Checkpoints | ❌ | ⚠️ | 🔨 Manual |
| **Observability** | ✅ LangSmith integration | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ❌ |
| **Production maturity** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | Depends |
| **Interview signal** | 🔥 Industry standard | 🤔 | 🤔 | 🔥 If done well |

> **Decision: LangGraph** — Purpose-built for stateful, multi-step agent workflows with explicit graph-based control flow. Supports streaming, checkpointing, and LangSmith observability out of the box. This is the most appropriate framework for an agent that needs to plan → execute → reason → synthesize.

### 3.5 LLM Provider & Model Selection

| Model | Use Case | Cost (per 1M tokens) | Speed | Quality |
|-------|----------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| **GPT-4o-mini** | Tool calls, data extraction, summarization | $0.15 in / $0.60 out | Fast | Good |
| **GPT-4o** | Final report synthesis, complex reasoning | $2.50 in / $10.00 out | Medium | Excellent |
| **Claude 3.5 Sonnet** | Fallback for synthesis | $3.00 in / $15.00 out | Medium | Excellent |

#### Multi-Model Routing Strategy

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MODEL ROUTING                       │
│                                                  │
│  Planning & tool selection  → GPT-4o-mini        │
│  Data extraction/parsing    → GPT-4o-mini        │
│  News summarization         → GPT-4o-mini        │
│  Financial analysis         → GPT-4o-mini        │
│  Final report synthesis     → GPT-4o             │
│  Complex reasoning/thesis   → GPT-4o             │
│                                                  │
│  Fallback (if OpenAI down)  → Claude 3.5 Sonnet  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Estimated Cost Per Report

| Component | Tokens (est.) | Model | Cost |
|-----------|--------------|-------|------|
| Planning (2-3 calls) | ~2,000 in / ~500 out | GPT-4o-mini | $0.0006 |
| Tool result processing (5-7 calls) | ~8,000 in / ~2,000 out | GPT-4o-mini | $0.0024 |
| Report synthesis (1 call) | ~6,000 in / ~3,000 out | GPT-4o | $0.045 |
| **Total per report** | **~16,000 in / ~5,500 out** | **Mixed** | **~$0.05** |

> [!TIP]
> At ~$0.05 per report, a reviewer can run 100 reports during evaluation for $5. This is an excellent cost profile to highlight.

### 3.6 Database — PostgreSQL

| | PostgreSQL | SQLite | MongoDB | Supabase |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|----------|
| **Relational queries** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **JSON support** | ✅ JSONB | ✅ JSON1 | ✅ Native | ✅ |
| **Production readiness** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ (single-user) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Docker-friendly** | ✅ | ✅ (file-based) | ✅ | ❌ External |
| **ORM support** | ✅ SQLAlchemy | ✅ SQLAlchemy | ⚠️ ODM | ✅ |

> **Decision: PostgreSQL (Docker) with SQLite fallback (local dev)**
> - PostgreSQL for Docker Compose deployment (production-like)
> - SQLite for quick local development without Docker
> - SQLAlchemy 2.0 abstracts the difference; same code works with both

### 3.7 Streaming — Server-Sent Events (SSE)

| | SSE | WebSocket | Long Polling | gRPC Streaming |
|---|-----|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Complexity** | ⭐ Simple | ⭐⭐⭐ Complex | ⭐⭐ Hacky | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Overkill |
| **Unidirectional** | ✅ Perfect for our use case | ⚠️ Bidirectional (overkill) | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Browser support** | ✅ Native EventSource | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Reconnection** | ✅ Built-in | ❌ Manual | ❌ Manual | ❌ |
| **FastAPI support** | ✅ StreamingResponse | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |

> **Decision: SSE (Server-Sent Events)** — Our use case is unidirectional: server pushes agent progress updates to the client. SSE is simpler than WebSocket, has built-in reconnection, and works natively with `fetch()` and `EventSource`. No need for bidirectional communication.

### 3.8 Data Retrieval Libraries

| Library | Purpose | Installation |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| **yfinance** | Stock prices, financials, company info, analyst data | `pip install yfinance` |
| **requests/httpx** | HTTP calls to FMP, Finnhub, NewsAPI, FRED, SEC EDGAR | `pip install httpx` |

> [!NOTE]
> We use `httpx` (async HTTP client) instead of `requests` for non-blocking API calls within the async FastAPI/LangGraph pipeline. `yfinance` is synchronous but will be wrapped in `asyncio.to_thread()` for async compatibility.

### 3.9 Testing Stack

| Tool | Purpose | Layer |
|------|---------|-------|
| **pytest** | Unit & integration tests | Backend |
| **pytest-asyncio** | Async test support | Backend |
| **pytest-cov** | Coverage reporting | Backend |
| **respx** | Mock HTTP responses (for httpx) | Backend |
| **Vitest** | Unit tests | Frontend |
| **Playwright** | E2E browser tests (optional) | Full stack |

### 3.10 Code Quality & DX

| Tool | Purpose | Config |
|------|---------|--------|
| **Ruff** | Python linting + formatting (replaces black, isort, flake8) | `ruff.toml` |
| **mypy** | Python static type checking | `pyproject.toml` |
| **ESLint** | TypeScript linting | `eslint.config.js` |
| **Prettier** | TypeScript/CSS formatting | `.prettierrc` |
| **pre-commit** | Git hooks for quality checks | `.pre-commit-config.yaml` |

### 3.11 Observability

| Tool | Purpose | Why |
|------|---------|-----|
| **LangSmith** | LLM call tracing, agent step visualization | Free tier; integrates with LangGraph natively |
| **structlog** | Structured JSON logging | Better than plain `logging`; machine-parseable |
| **Python `time`** | Request latency tracking | Simple and effective |

### 3.12 Deployment

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Docker** | Containerize backend and frontend |
| **Docker Compose** | Orchestrate backend + frontend + PostgreSQL + Redis |
| **`.env.example`** | Template for required environment variables |
| **Makefile** | Common commands: `make dev`, `make test`, `make docker-up` |

---

## 4. Dependency Manifests

### Backend — `pyproject.toml` (Key Dependencies)

```toml
[project]
name = "ai-investment-agent"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.12"

dependencies = [
    # Web framework
    "fastapi>=0.115.0",
    "uvicorn[standard]>=0.30.0",
    "sse-starlette>=2.1.0",
    
    # AI / Agent
    "langgraph>=0.2.0",
    "langchain>=0.3.0",
    "langchain-openai>=0.2.0",
    "langchain-community>=0.3.0",
    
    # Data retrieval
    "yfinance>=0.2.40",
    "httpx>=0.27.0",
    
    # Database
    "sqlalchemy[asyncio]>=2.0.30",
    "alembic>=1.13.0",
    "asyncpg>=0.29.0",       # PostgreSQL async driver
    "aiosqlite>=0.20.0",     # SQLite async driver
    
    # Validation & serialization
    "pydantic>=2.7.0",
    "pydantic-settings>=2.3.0",
    
    # Utilities
    "structlog>=24.4.0",
    "python-dotenv>=1.0.0",
    "tenacity>=8.4.0",       # Retry logic
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.2.0",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.23.0",
    "pytest-cov>=5.0.0",
    "respx>=0.21.0",
    "ruff>=0.5.0",
    "mypy>=1.10.0",
    "pre-commit>=3.7.0",
]
```

### Frontend — `package.json` (Key Dependencies)

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "next": "^15.0.0",
    "react": "^19.0.0",
    "react-dom": "^19.0.0",
    "zustand": "^5.0.0",
    "recharts": "^2.12.0",
    "react-markdown": "^9.0.0",
    "lucide-react": "^0.400.0",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.0",
    "clsx": "^2.1.0",
    "tailwind-merge": "^2.4.0",
    "date-fns": "^3.6.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "typescript": "^5.5.0",
    "@types/react": "^19.0.0",
    "tailwindcss": "^4.0.0",
    "@tailwindcss/postcss": "^4.0.0",
    "eslint": "^9.0.0",
    "eslint-config-next": "^15.0.0",
    "prettier": "^3.3.0",
    "vitest": "^2.0.0"
  }
}
```

---

## 5. Environment Variables

```bash
# .env.example

# LLM
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
OPENAI_MODEL_PRIMARY=gpt-4o-mini          # For tool calls, extraction
OPENAI_MODEL_SYNTHESIS=gpt-4o             # For final report generation

# Data APIs (optional — yfinance needs no key)
FMP_API_KEY=...                            # Financial Modeling Prep
FINNHUB_API_KEY=...                        # Finnhub (news)
NEWS_API_KEY=...                           # NewsAPI (backup news source)

# Database
DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@localhost:5432/investment_agent
# DATABASE_URL=sqlite+aiosqlite:///./data/investment_agent.db  # SQLite fallback

# Observability
LANGSMITH_API_KEY=...                      # Optional but recommended
LANGSMITH_PROJECT=ai-investment-agent

# App
APP_ENV=development                        # development | production
LOG_LEVEL=INFO
MAX_AGENT_STEPS=15                         # Circuit breaker
TOKEN_BUDGET=20000                         # Max tokens per research session
```

---

## 6. Architecture Fit Validation

### Does the Stack Support Every Must-Have Feature?

| Must-Have Feature | Stack Component | Supported? |
|------------------|----------------|------------|
| M1: Ticker input & validation | FastAPI + Pydantic | ✅ |
| M2: Multi-step agent (LangGraph) | LangGraph | ✅ |
| M3: Stock price retrieval | yfinance | ✅ |
| M4: Financial statements | yfinance + FMP | ✅ |
| M5: Key metrics calculation | Python + Pydantic | ✅ |
| M6: News retrieval | Finnhub + NewsAPI | ✅ |
| M7: Company profile | yfinance | ✅ |
| M8: Structured report generation | LangGraph + GPT-4o | ✅ |
| M9: Report sections | Pydantic output schemas | ✅ |
| M10: Source citations | Agent tool design | ✅ |
| M11: REST API backend | FastAPI | ✅ |
| M12: Streaming progress | SSE + LangGraph astream | ✅ |
| M13: Polished frontend | Next.js + shadcn/ui | ✅ |
| M14: Agent reasoning trace | LangGraph callbacks + SSE | ✅ |
| M15: Error handling & degradation | FastAPI middleware + tenacity | ✅ |

✅ **All 15 Must-Have features are fully supported by this stack.**

---

## 7. Technology Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| **yfinance breaks** (it scrapes Yahoo; can be flaky) | Fallback to FMP for all financial data |
| **LangGraph API changes** (relatively new library) | Pin version; isolate behind abstraction |
| **OpenAI rate limits / outage** | Retry with tenacity; fallback to Claude |
| **Next.js 15 App Router complexity** | Keep frontend simple; minimal server-side logic |
| **PostgreSQL overhead for demo** | SQLite fallback for reviewers who skip Docker |
| **shadcn/ui version mismatch** | Pin components at init time; they're copied, not imported |

---

## Summary: Why This Stack

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  WHY THIS STACK                         │
│                                                         │
│  FastAPI    → Best Python async web framework           │
│  LangGraph  → Purpose-built for stateful AI agents      │
│  Next.js    → Industry-standard React framework         │
│  shadcn/ui  → Premium UI with zero overhead             │
│  PostgreSQL → Production-grade persistence              │
│  SSE        → Simple, effective streaming               │
│  GPT-4o     → Best quality-to-cost for synthesis        │
│  yfinance   → Free, zero-auth financial data            │
│  Docker     → One-command deployment for reviewers      │
│                                                         │
│  Every choice is:                                       │
│  ✦ Industry standard (reviewers recognize it)           │
│  ✦ Production-grade (not a toy)                         │
│  ✦ Well-documented (you can learn it fast)              │
│  ✦ Interview-defensible ("I chose X because...")        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next Step**: Proceed to **Step 5 — System Architecture** to design the high-level architecture: component diagram, data flow, communication patterns, and how every piece connects end-to-end.

Defines the complete system design with 8 Mermaid diagrams covering: high-level architecture (3-tier + AI layer), request lifecycle (12-step data flow from user input to rendered report), LangGraph agent state machine (7 nodes: planning, data gathering, analysis, synthesis, review, report generation, error handling), streaming architecture (SSE event types and client-side handling), caching strategy (3-tier: in-memory LRU, HTTP response cache, database cache with TTLs), error handling architecture (retry policies, circuit breakers, graceful degradation matrix), component communication patterns, and Docker deployment topology. Each architectural decision is justified with trade-off analysis.
